title: Ping Identity OAuth2 keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Ping Identity OAuth2 client ID detected. OAuth2 client IDs are used to identify applications in PingFederate, PingAccess, and PingOne authorization flows. These identifiers are part of the OAuth2/OIDC authentication process and when exposed alongside client secrets, enable unauthorized access to OAuth2-protected resources. While client IDs alone are not sufficient for authentication, exposing them reveals application architecture and OAuth2 configuration details that can be leveraged by attackers. Client IDs should be stored as environment variables or in configuration management systems, never hardcoded in source code. Review OAuth2 client registration and implement secure credential management practices.

Ping Identity OAuth2 client secret detected. OAuth2 client secrets are confidential credentials used by applications to authenticate with PingFederate, PingAccess, and PingOne OAuth2/OIDC token endpoints. These secrets are the cryptographic keys that prove an application's identity during OAuth2 authorization code exchange, client credentials flow, and token refresh operations. Exposed client secrets allow attackers to: impersonate legitimate applications, obtain access tokens for protected APIs, access user data without authorization, bypass OAuth2 security controls, compromise SSO sessions, and escalate privileges within federated identity systems. This represents a critical security vulnerability in identity and access management infrastructure. Immediately revoke the exposed client secret through PingFederate admin console or PingOne portal, generate new credentials, update all application configurations, audit OAuth2 access logs for unauthorized token requests, and implement secure secrets management using HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. Never commit client secrets to version control.

Ping Identity OAuth2 client ID explicitly assigned. Detected OAuth2 client identifier in assignment or configuration statement for PingFederate, PingAccess, or PingOne. This pattern indicates deliberate OAuth2 client configuration with a hardcoded identifier. While client IDs are less sensitive than secrets, hardcoding them in source code exposes OAuth2 application architecture, makes credential rotation difficult, and often appears alongside hardcoded client secrets. Use environment variables (PING_CLIENT_ID) or configuration files outside version control. Review adjacent code for potential client secret exposure.

Ping Identity OAuth2 client secret explicitly assigned - CRITICAL EXPOSURE. Detected hardcoded OAuth2 client secret in assignment statement for PingFederate, PingAccess, or PingOne OAuth2 client. This is a critical security vulnerability that grants full OAuth2 client authentication capabilities. Attackers can use this secret to: obtain access tokens for any authorized scope, impersonate the legitimate application, access protected APIs and user data, compromise OAuth2 authorization flows, bypass authentication controls, and potentially escalate to full account takeover. IMMEDIATE ACTION REQUIRED: (1) Revoke this client secret through PingFederate admin API or PingOne console, (2) Generate new client credentials, (3) Update application configuration with environment variable, (4) Audit OAuth2 access logs for unauthorized usage, (5) Implement secrets scanning in CI/CD pipeline. Store client secrets in secure vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) or as encrypted environment variables. Never commit to version control.
